episode_meta.title: Melissa Bell, Aron Pilhofer, Mark Chonofsky & David Chivers: Chicago Public Media on Building AI Tools That Serve the Audience

ai_tools: AI-powered translation tools (unspecified, used for English-to-Spanish translation)
AI-enabled web development tools (used to build natural-language-to-SQL query interface)
Slack integrations (AI-generated weather and traffic reports in the voice of WBEZ)
Figma Make (AI-backed design tool, mentioned by Aron Pilhofer for prototyping)
MCP (Model Context Protocol, referenced as emerging transformational tool from OpenAI)
AI coding assistants (used by Mark Chonofsky for writing code)
ChatGPT / generative AI (used by Aron Pilhofer for coding, prototyping, vendor comparison)
Machine learning models (for content recommendation, distinguished from LLM-based approaches)
Content recommendation vendors (third-party, chosen over building in-house)

use_cases.description: Transcribing an extensive audio archive from WBEZ radio to make decades of audio content searchable, discoverable, and citable alongside the print archive
use_cases.outcome: Unlocks 40+ years of audio journalism for discoverability; enables audio sources to appear in print pieces; creates new opportunities for historical archival work

use_cases.description: Translating editorial content from English to Spanish to better serve Spanish-speaking audiences in the Chicagoland area
use_cases.outcome: Dramatically faster turnaround from 'yesterday's news tomorrow' to near-real-time; previously translated content was essentially last week's news by the time it was published

use_cases.description: Building a natural-language interface for a journalist to query a large spreadsheet of Chicago schools data without needing to know SQL
use_cases.tool_used: AI-enabled web development tools (natural language to SQL)
use_cases.outcome: Enabled a journalist to query a complex dataset conversationally; created a reusable data access tool pattern for similar future needs

use_cases.description: Slack integrations delivering weather and traffic reports in the editorial voice of WBEZ
use_cases.outcome: Quick proof of concept that built trust with on-air talent and newsroom staff; demonstrated AI can respect editorial voice

use_cases.description: Content recommendation to improve recirculation and surface relevant content to individual audience members
use_cases.tool_used: Initially proposed LLM-based approach; pivoted to third-party vendors using traditional machine learning techniques
use_cases.outcome: Decision that LLM inference was not the right approach; consensus to use external vendors with better models at lower staff cost

use_cases.description: Using AI for rapid prototyping and demos instead of lengthy written memos to accelerate product development cycles
use_cases.tool_used: Figma Make, AI coding tools, generative AI (unspecified)
use_cases.outcome: Product managers can produce functional demos of potential features rather than writing 40-page memos; faster product cycles

use_cases.description: Using AI to compare and contrast technology vendor feature sets, API robustness, and capabilities during platform assessment
Planned use of AI to parse massive data sets for deep investigative reporting
use_cases.outcome: Expected to make investigative journalism more efficient for journalists

use_cases.description: Planned AI tools for local business outreach to better understand businesses for underwriting campaigns
use_cases.outcome: Expected to deepen connection to local community and strengthen membership business

key_claims.claim: Chicago Public Media has both public media radio assets (WBEZ) and a traditional print/web publication (Chicago Sun-Times), making it a unique institution in the Lenfest collaborative
key_claims.speaker: David Chivers

key_claims.claim: The journalism industry had mixed results adapting to the internet's disruption and missed many opportunities; it is critical not to repeat that mistake with AI
key_claims.speaker: Melissa Bell

key_claims.claim: AI translation reduced turnaround from 'yesterday's news tomorrow' (essentially last week's news) to much faster publication for Spanish-language content
key_claims.speaker: David Chivers

key_claims.claim: Chicago Public Media's audio archive is so extensive they don't actually know how much they have
key_claims.speaker: Mark Chonofsky

key_claims.claim: Content recommendation should not be done using LLM inference; traditional machine learning and third-party vendors are better suited
key_claims.speaker: Mark Chonofsky

key_claims.claim: The product team at Chicago Public Media consists of only two product managers, the minimum to even call it a team
key_claims.speaker: Aron Pilhofer

key_claims.claim: MCP (Model Context Protocol) is going to be transformational for modular AI integration work in newsrooms
key_claims.speaker: David Chivers

key_claims.claim: In the Chicago Sun-Times AI-generated book list incident (May 2025), 7 out of 10 recommended books were hallucinated (imaginary titles by real authors), created by a Hearst freelancer using AI against policy
key_claims.speaker: Melissa Bell

key_claims.claim: People's reluctance and fear around AI comes from being presented with AI solutions to problems they don't have or problems not well-solved by AI
key_claims.speaker: Mark Chonofsky

key_claims.claim: The Lenfest AI Fellows independently organized their own biweekly working group meetings within the first month of the program
key_claims.speaker: David Chivers

challenges.description: Limited resources at local and public media organizations constrain experimentation with AI; tight budgets leave little room for exploration beyond editorial, product, and maintenance needs
Building trust with newsroom staff as a non-journalist AI fellow; newsrooms are tricky places for outsiders and require proving value through small, impactful wins
People's reluctance and fear around AI stems from previous experiences where someone pushed an AI solution to a problem they didn't have or that AI doesn't solve well

The AI-generated book list incident (Chicago Sun-Times, May 2025) demonstrated how AI hallucinations can slip through multiple editorial safeguards and go viral, overshadowing the organization's real investigative journalism
Deciding what to build in-house versus buy from vendors, especially with only one AI fellow and an overburdened product team of two
The AI space moves so fast that original grant proposals can become obsolete before implementation; need for flexibility to pivot when vendors already offer proposed solutions
Ensuring generative AI is used with guardrails and never in the business of generating content, including links, while still finding the useful space around it

Traditional recommendation engines provide 'more of the same' rather than truly relevant, individually targeted content; building better personalization without the 'semi-embarrassing' approaches other news organizations have tried

quotes.text: I think the journalism industry had mixed results on adapting to the internet's disruption of journalism and missed a lot of opportunity to take their journalism forward, their distribution forward, their community gathering forward with the new tools available to them. So I think it's really important now for us to not miss this new opportunity where everything is almost being rewritten in the technology space.
quotes.speaker: Melissa Bell
quotes.topic: Learning from past failures of digital adaptation

quotes.text: People's reluctance and fear is around people who've come to them with an AI solution to a problem that they don't have or a problem that is not well solved by an AI system.
quotes.speaker: Mark Chonofsky

quotes.text: We're spending a lot of time ensuring that the role of generative AI specifically is that there are guardrails around it and that we are not in the business of generating content at any point, up to and including links. We're not interested in that. We're not focused on that. But around it is a space for us to experiment.
quotes.speaker: Aron Pilhofer
quotes.topic: Guardrails and boundaries for generative AI in newsrooms

quotes.text: I wouldn't say that we have an AI product roadmap. I would say we have a product roadmap of which AI is a part. AI is contributing and enabling us to conceive of and launch potentially God willing successful products that we wouldn't otherwise be able to launch.
quotes.speaker: Aron Pilhofer
quotes.topic: Integrating AI into product strategy rather than treating it as separate

quotes.text: One of the both the opportunities that AI presents to us is better surfacing our humanity and better surfacing our authenticity. And a lot of times I think there's a tendency, especially in leadership roles, to be a little bit more buttoned up and not admit to mistakes... I think that this is a moment for us as leaders to reconsider that and to say we need to admit that we're humans and that we err and that there is going to be a learning curve for us just as much as there is for anyone else.
quotes.speaker: Melissa Bell
quotes.topic: Leadership transparency and responding to AI failures

quotes.text: I came to this job as really an AI skeptic and I think I still maintain a skeptical mindset. I don't use AI tools in at least the popular sense in my personal life at all, use it extensively for writing code for work and in the ways that it's integrated into our workflow processes... But you know, as soon as I get home, I'm like, I don't deal with this anymore.
quotes.speaker: Mark Chonofsky
quotes.topic: Healthy skepticism from the AI practitioner's perspective

recommendations.advice: Start with small, impactful proof-of-concept projects (like Slack integrations) to build trust before pursuing larger enterprise-level AI deployments
recommendations.speaker: David Chivers
recommendations.target_audience: Newsrooms and AI fellows beginning AI adoption

recommendations.advice: Get to know people and their workflows personally; don't push AI solutions but instead make yourself available when people hit tedious barriers that AI can genuinely help with
recommendations.speaker: Mark Chonofsky
recommendations.target_audience: AI fellows and technologists entering newsrooms

recommendations.advice: Do not create a separate AI product roadmap; integrate AI as an enabling component within the broader product roadmap
recommendations.speaker: Aron Pilhofer

recommendations.advice: Build in-house only when the use case is specific enough to your situation that you'll stay ahead of competitors or when it's too small for outside vendors to develop; otherwise, buy from vendors
recommendations.speaker: Mark Chonofsky

recommendations.advice: Produce demos, not memos: use AI-enabled prototyping to quickly show what a product could look like rather than writing lengthy specifications
recommendations.speaker: Aron Pilhofer

recommendations.advice: When AI failures happen publicly, respond with authenticity and transparency; use the incident as an opportunity to establish clear AI policies, apologize to the audience, and create space for internal dialogue about responsible AI use
recommendations.speaker: Melissa Bell

recommendations.advice: Create a community of practice among AI fellows with structured biweekly meetings that include project demos, problem-solving, and tracking broader AI industry developments
recommendations.speaker: Mark Chonofsky

recommendations.advice: Be flexible and willing to pivot from original grant proposals when the AI landscape changes or when vendors already offer what you planned to build
recommendations.speaker: David Chivers
